Summary: In CD45.2 Sirt7−/− pro-B cells transplanted into sublethally irradiated CD45.1/CD45.2 mice, Pax5 deacetylation at K198 was required for B cell development. Expression of Pax5 K198R or Pax5 wild-type in Sirt7−/− pro-B cells reversed the reduction in splenic B220+ CD19+ GFP+ B cells and restored repression of 93 upregulated genes, including Cxcr5 and Tet1. Pax5 wild-type mainly repressed lineage-inappropriate genes. The results indicated that dynamic acetylation and deacetylation of Pax5 were required for B cell development and that Pax5 deacetylation was necessary to restrict lineage plasticity. In Pax5−/− pro-B cells, constitutively deacetylated Pax5 K198R abrogated T cell potential and failed to restore B cell development. This observation indicated that Pax5 promotes lineage restriction and B cell development through independent mechanisms. Neither acetylated nor deacetylated Pax5 mimics rescued B cell differentiation, which implied that both forms are required for lymphopoiesis. The deacetylated mimic repressed lineage plasticity and overactivated Igh recombination genes. The deacetylated mimic also failed to induce normal clonal expansion programs. Modulation of acetylation at Pax5 K198 acted as a regulatory switch, with K198-deacetylated Pax5 repressing lineage-inappropriate and cytokine signaling genes. K198-acetylated Pax5 failed to repress lineage-inappropriate genes and more efficiently regulated genes linked to V(D)J recombination and proliferation. Binding of Pax5 K198Q and Pax5 K198R to promoter or proximal enhancer regions correlated with induction or silencing of the bound genes.

A SIRT7-dependent acetylation switch regulates early B cell differentiation and lineage commitment through Pax5. Nat Immunol, 25(12), 2308-2319. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41590-024-01995-7
